curriculum.title: JavaScript: Intermediate to Professional
curriculum.description: Become a JavaScript expert! Master advanced patterns, performance, testing, and modern tooling. Learn how pros write clean, scalable, production-ready code. You're ready to level up!

lessons.title: Module Pattern and ES6 Modules
lessons.body: Modules help you organize code into reusable, isolated files. The Module Pattern uses IIFEs for privacy. ES6 modules use import/export for clean dependency management. Both prevent global namespace pollution.
lessons.avatar_script: Think of modules like rooms in a house: each has a purpose, and you only bring in what you need. No more cluttered global space! Let's build clean, maintainable code.

code_example.description: ES6 module syntax: named and default exports/imports

questions.question: What does 'export default' allow?
options: One primary export that can be imported without curly braces
questions.answer: One primary export that can be imported without curly braces
questions.explanation: Default exports are imported without { }, making them convenient for the main export of a module.

questions.question: ES6 modules are statically analyzed at compile time.
questions.explanation: Yes! Import/export are resolved before code runs, enabling better tree-shaking and optimization.

questions.question: Export a function 'greet' as the default export
questions.explanation: Use 'export default' before your function declaration to make it the primary export of the module.

code_example.description: Default export example
code_example.explanation: Begin by writing export default function greet(name) {. The words export default make this function the main value being sent out from the file. Inside the brackets, add the parameter name because the function needs a name to greet. On the next line, write return `Hello, ${name}!`; so the function sends back a greeting message. Then close the function with a curly bracket. This allows another file to import the function without using curly braces.

lessons.title: Prototypes and Classical Inheritance
lessons.body: JavaScript uses prototypal inheritance: objects can inherit properties/methods from other objects. The prototype chain lets you share behavior efficiently. Understanding this unlocks powerful patterns.
lessons.avatar_script: Imagine a family tree: children inherit traits from parents, who inherited from grandparents. JavaScript's prototype chain works similarly - objects share behavior up the chain!

code_example.description: Prototypal inheritance with constructor functions

questions.question: What does Object.create(proto) do?
options: Creates a new object with proto as its prototype
questions.answer: Creates a new object with proto as its prototype
questions.explanation: Object.create() sets up the prototype chain directly, which is key for inheritance.

questions.question: Methods on the prototype are shared across all instances.
questions.explanation: Yes! This saves memory because all instances reference the same function, not copies.

questions.question: Add a method 'describe' to the prototype of 'Car'
questions.explanation: Assign a function to Constructor.prototype.methodName to share methods across all instances of that constructor.

code_example.description: Adding a method to a constructor's prototype
code_example.explanation: Begin by making sure the Car constructor already exists. After that, write Car.prototype.describe = function() {. This adds a shared method called describe to all Car objects created from the constructor. Inside the function body, write return `${this.make} ${this.model}`;. The word this refers to the current car object, so this.make and this.model access its values. Finally, close the function and add a semicolon.

lessons.title: Classes and Modern OOP
lessons.body: ES6 classes provide cleaner syntax for prototypes. Use class, constructor, extends, and super for inheritance. Add static methods, getters/setters, and private fields (#) for encapsulation.
lessons.avatar_script: Classes are like blueprints for objects. They make inheritance and encapsulation clearer. But remember: under the hood, it's still prototypes - just with nicer syntax!

code_example.description: Modern class syntax with inheritance, private fields, and static methods

questions.question: What does 'super()' do in a subclass constructor?
questions.explanation: super() initializes the parent part of the object. It must be called before using 'this' in the subclass.

questions.question: Private fields (#field) are accessible only within the class.
questions.explanation: Yes! # syntax creates truly private fields that can't be accessed from outside the class.

questions.question: Create a getter for a private field '#balance'
questions.explanation: Use the 'get' keyword to create a getter method that returns the value of the private field with the # prefix.

code_example.description: Getter for private field
code_example.explanation: Begin by creating a class, such as class Account {. Inside the class, declare the private field by writing #balance = 0;. The # symbol makes the field private so it can only be used inside the class. On the next line, write get balance() { to begin the getter. Inside the getter, write return this.#balance; so the getter sends back the private value. Then close the getter and the class. This lets outside code read the value through balance without directly touching #balance.

lessons.title: Pure Functions and Immutability
lessons.body: Pure functions always return the same output for the same input and have no side effects. Immutability means never changing data, only creating new versions. Together, they make code predictable and testable.
lessons.avatar_script: Think of pure functions like math: 2+2 always equals 4, no matter what. No hidden changes, no surprises. This makes debugging and testing so much easier!

code_example.description: Contrasting impure vs pure functions and immutable updates

questions.question: What makes a function 'pure'?
options: It has no side effects and is deterministic
questions.answer: It has no side effects and is deterministic
questions.explanation: Pure functions depend only on inputs and don't modify external state - making them reliable and testable.

questions.question: map() creates a new array without modifying the original.
questions.explanation: Yes! Array methods like map, filter, and slice return new arrays, supporting immutability.

questions.question: Write a pure function that adds tax to a price without modifying inputs
questions.explanation: A pure function only uses its parameters and returns a new value. Multiply price by (1 + rate) and return the result.

code_example.description: Pure function example
code_example.explanation: Begin by writing a function header such as function addTax(price, rate) {. The function should take price and rate as inputs so it does not depend on outside values. Inside the function, write return price * (1 + rate);. This creates and returns a new calculated value instead of changing any original data. After that, close the function. Because the function only uses its inputs and returns a result, it stays pure.

lessons.title: Higher-Order Functions and Composition
lessons.body: Higher-order functions take functions as arguments or return them. Composition combines small functions to build complex behavior. This enables powerful, reusable patterns like middleware and pipelines.
lessons.avatar_script: Imagine LEGO blocks: each function is a small piece. Higher-order functions let you snap them together in creative ways. Composition is your instruction manual for building big things from small parts!

code_example.description: Higher-order functions and function composition with pipe

questions.question: What is a higher-order function?
options: A function that only works with arrays
A function that takes or returns other functions
questions.answer: A function that takes or returns other functions
questions.explanation: Higher-order functions treat functions as values - enabling powerful abstractions and reuse.

questions.question: Function composition applies functions right-to-left by default.
questions.explanation: It depends on implementation! Our pipe() example applies left-to-right. Compose() often does right-to-left.

questions.question: Create a function that returns another function adding a fixed value
questions.explanation: Use currying: the outer function takes the fixed value, returns an inner function that adds that value to its input.

code_example.description: Curried add function example
code_example.explanation: Begin by writing an outer function that takes a fixed value, such as const add = (x) =>. After that arrow, return another function by writing (y) => x + y. The outer function stores the fixed value x, and the inner function receives a new value y later. When the inner function runs, it adds y to the saved value x. You can then create a new function like const add5 = add(5); and test it with console.log(add5(3));.

lessons.title: Recursion and Advanced Iteration
lessons.body: Recursion is when a function calls itself to solve smaller versions of a problem. It's elegant for trees, nested data, and mathematical sequences. Always define a base case to avoid infinite loops!
lessons.avatar_script: Recursion is like a set of Russian dolls: each doll contains a smaller one, until you reach the tiniest doll (the base case). Then you build back up with the answers!

code_example.description: Recursive functions for factorial and array flattening

questions.question: What is the base case in recursion?
options: The condition that stops further recursion
questions.answer: The condition that stops further recursion
questions.explanation: The base case prevents infinite recursion by providing a direct answer for the simplest input.

questions.question: Every recursive function must have a base case.
questions.explanation: Yes! Without a base case, recursion would continue forever and crash with a stack overflow.

questions.question: Write a recursive function that sums numbers from 1 to n
questions.explanation: Define a base case (return 0 when n is 0) and a recursive case (return n plus the sum of 1 to n-1).

code_example.description: Recursive sum function
code_example.explanation: Begin by writing a function header such as function sumTo(n) {. Inside the function, start with a base case by writing if (n <= 0) return 0;. This stops the function from calling itself forever. After that, write return n + sumTo(n - 1);. This is the recursive part, where the function calls itself with a smaller number each time. Finally, close the function. When you call sumTo(5), it adds 5 + 4 + 3 + 2 + 1.

lessons.title: Optimizing JavaScript Performance
lessons.body: Performance matters! Use debouncing/throttling for frequent events, memoization for expensive calculations, and lazy loading for resources. Profile with DevTools to find real bottlenecks, not guesses.
lessons.avatar_script: Think of performance like a race car: you don't upgrade everything - you find the slowest part and fix that first. Profiling tells you where to focus your optimization efforts!

code_example.description: Debouncing and memoization for performance optimization

questions.question: What does debouncing do?
options: Runs a function immediately on every event
Delays function execution until events stop
Runs a function at fixed intervals
questions.answer: Delays function execution until events stop
questions.explanation: Debouncing waits for a pause in rapid events before running the function - perfect for search inputs or resizing.

questions.question: Memoization trades memory for speed by caching results.
questions.explanation: Yes! Memoization stores previous results to avoid recalculating - great for pure functions with expensive operations.

questions.question: Write a simple debounce function that delays execution by 'ms' milliseconds
questions.explanation: Store a timer ID, clear any existing timer on each call, then set a new timeout to run the function after the delay.

code_example.description: Basic debounce implementation
code_example.explanation: Begin by writing a function named debounce that accepts fn and ms as parameters. Inside the function, create a variable such as let timeout; to store the timer id. Next, return a new function that can receive any arguments by writing return function(...args) {. Inside that returned function, first write clearTimeout(timeout); so any old timer is removed. Then write timeout = setTimeout(() => fn.apply(this, args), ms);. This creates a new timer and waits for the number of milliseconds stored in ms before calling fn. Finally, close both functions.

lessons.title: Testing with Jest: Unit and Integration
lessons.body: Testing ensures your code works now and later. Jest is a popular test runner. Write unit tests for small functions, integration tests for combined parts. Use describe, test/it, and expect for clear, maintainable tests.
lessons.avatar_script: Testing is like a safety net for your code: it catches bugs before users do. Write tests as you code, and you'll refactor with confidence. Let's make your code bulletproof!

code_example.description: Jest unit tests with describe, test, and expect assertions

questions.question: What does expect(value).toBe(expected) check?
questions.explanation: toBe() uses strict equality (===). For objects, use toEqual() for deep comparison.

questions.question: describe() groups related tests together.
questions.explanation: Yes! describe() creates test suites for better organization and reporting.

questions.question: Write a Jest test that checks if multiply(3, 4) equals 12
questions.explanation: Use test() with a description, then expect(multiply(3, 4)).toBe(12) to assert the function returns the correct value.

code_example.description: Simple Jest test example
code_example.explanation: Begin by writing test( and give the test a clear name, such as 'multiply returns product'. After the comma, write a callback function using () => {. Inside the callback, write expect(multiply(3, 4)).toBe(12);. The expect() part runs the function, and .toBe(12) checks that the result is exactly 12. Then close the callback and the test function. This creates one Jest test for the multiply function.

lessons.title: Modern Tooling: Bundlers, Linters, and TypeScript
lessons.body: Professional JavaScript uses tools: Webpack/Vite bundle code, ESLint/Prettier enforce style, TypeScript adds static types. These catch errors early, improve collaboration, and scale with your team.
lessons.avatar_script: Tooling is like a pro chef's kitchen: sharp knives (linters), organized stations (bundlers), and recipe notes (TypeScript). The right tools make great code easier to write and maintain!

code_example.description: TypeScript interfaces and tooling configuration examples

questions.question: What does TypeScript add to JavaScript?
options: Static type checking at compile time
questions.answer: Static type checking at compile time
questions.explanation: TypeScript adds types that are checked before code runs, catching many errors during development.

questions.question: ESLint can automatically fix some code style issues.
questions.explanation: Yes! With --fix flag, ESLint can auto-correct many formatting and style problems.

questions.question: Define a TypeScript interface 'Product' with 'id' (number) and 'name' (string)
questions.explanation: Use the interface keyword followed by the name, then define each property with its type using colon syntax.

code_example.description: Simple TypeScript interface
code_example.explanation: Begin by writing interface Product { on one line. Inside the interface, add id: number; to describe the id property and show that it must store a number. On the next line, add name: string; to describe the name property and show that it must store text. After that, close the interface with a curly bracket. This tells TypeScript that any Product object must include both id and name with the correct types.

lessons.title: State Management Patterns
lessons.body: As apps grow, managing state gets complex. Patterns like Observer, Redux-style stores, or Context API help keep state predictable. The key: single source of truth, unidirectional data flow, and pure updates.
lessons.avatar_script: State management is like air traffic control: many moving parts, but clear rules prevent collisions. Good patterns keep your app predictable even as it scales!

code_example.description: Observable store pattern with immutable updates and pure reducer

questions.question: Why return a copy of state in getState()?
options: To prevent external code from mutating internal state
questions.answer: To prevent external code from mutating internal state
questions.explanation: Returning a copy enforces immutability - a core principle for predictable state management.

questions.question: Reducers should be pure functions.
questions.explanation: Yes! Pure reducers make state changes predictable, testable, and debuggable.

questions.question: Write a pure reducer case that updates 'count' by adding payload
questions.explanation: Return a new state object using spread syntax, then update count by adding action.payload to the existing value.

code_example.code:
case 'INCREMENT':
  return { ...state, count: state.count + action.payload };
code_example.description: Pure reducer case example
code_example.explanation: Begin by writing the reducer case label, such as case 'INCREMENT':. On the next line, write return { ...state, count: state.count + action.payload };. The ...state part copies the existing state into a new object. Then count: state.count + action.payload creates an updated count value without changing the old state directly. This keeps the reducer pure because it returns a new state object instead of mutating the original one.

lessons.title: Error Boundaries and Resilient Design
lessons.body: Professional apps handle failures gracefully. Use error boundaries to catch UI errors, retry logic for network failures, and fallback UIs. Design for failure: assume things will go wrong, and plan for it.
lessons.avatar_script: Resilient design is like building a bridge: you expect strong winds and heavy traffic, so you add supports and safety margins. Your code should handle real-world chaos gracefully!

code_example.description: Error boundary pattern and retry logic for resilience

questions.question: What is the purpose of an error boundary?
options: Catch errors in child components and show fallback UI
Log errors to the console only
questions.answer: Catch errors in child components and show fallback UI
questions.explanation: Error boundaries isolate failures so one broken component doesn't crash the entire app.

questions.question: Exponential backoff increases wait time between retries.
questions.explanation: Yes! Waiting longer after each failure reduces load on struggling services and improves success rates.

questions.question: Write a simple retry wrapper that tries a function up to 3 times
questions.explanation: Use a for loop with try/catch inside. On each iteration, try to return the result. Only throw the error on the final attempt.

code_example.description: Basic retry wrapper function
code_example.explanation: Begin by writing an async function header such as async function retry(fn, attempts = 3) {. Inside the function, create a for loop that starts at 0 and continues while i < attempts. Inside the loop, add a try block and write return await fn(); so the function tries to run fn. After the try block, add a catch block. Inside catch, check whether this is the last attempt by writing if (i === attempts - 1) throw err;. This means the error will only be thrown after all retry attempts have failed. Then close the loop and the function.

lessons.title: Capstone: Build a Production-Ready Feature
lessons.body: Let's combine everything: build a searchable, paginated, cached data table with error handling, loading states, and tests. We'll use modules, async/await, pure functions, and professional patterns.
lessons.avatar_script: This is your graduation project! You'll apply every concept we've learned to build something real. Take your time, think through each decision, and trust your skills. You've earned this!

code_example.description: Production-ready pattern: cached async fetch with state management

questions.question: Why check 'cancelled' in the useEffect cleanup?
options: To prevent state updates on unmounted components
questions.answer: To prevent state updates on unmounted components
questions.explanation: Updating state after a component unmounts causes memory leaks and warnings. Cleanup prevents this.

questions.question: Caching fetch results reduces network requests and improves UX.
questions.explanation: Yes! Smart caching avoids redundant work, speeds up repeated views, and reduces server load.

questions.question: Write a cache check: if key exists in map, return cached value
questions.explanation: Use Map's has() method to check if the key exists, then get() to retrieve and return the cached value.

code_example.description: Simple cache lookup pattern
code_example.explanation: Begin by writing an if statement that checks whether the map already contains the key. Type if (cache.has(key)) {. Inside the curly brackets, write return cache.get(key);. The has() method checks whether the key exists, and get() retrieves the saved value for that key. After that, close the curly bracket. This pattern lets your program reuse stored data instead of doing the same work again.
